title: Macramé jewellery organiser
subtitle: A wall-mounted knotted holder with rings and hooks for jewellery storage
excerpt: A wall-mounted macramé panel with integrated curtain rings and small hooks for storing earrings, necklaces, and bracelets. Three hanging cord sections hold rings of varying sizes. Mounted on a 40 cm wooden dowel, the piece is both practical storage and a decorative object. Suitable for makers who have completed a basic plant hanger and are ready for structured panel work.
sourceNotes:
- General macramé practice: wall panel construction, ring integration, dowel mounting
- Standard curtain ring sizing for jewellery organiser use

glossaryTerms.definition: A simple mounting knot used to attach cords to a dowel, ring, or bar; folds the cord at its midpoint and passes both ends through the loop.
A symmetrical flat knot made over two central filler cords using two working cords; forms the basic unit of most macramé fabric.
A wrapping knot that binds a group of cords together tightly; used to consolidate cord ends or to secure a ring in place.
The passive central cord(s) over which working cords are knotted; the filler holds the shape and tension of the structure.

content.text: This wall panel organises everyday jewellery using three hanging columns of square-knot work, each holding a curtain ring at the planned position for displaying necklaces and bracelets. Small hooks added between the columns hold earrings. The whole piece mounts on a 40 cm wooden dowel using a
lark's head knot
row and hangs from a wall hook or nail. Plan to have the curtain rings to hand before you begin: the rings are threaded onto cords before knotting and cannot be added afterward.
Setup

You will need approximately 30 m of 3 mm cotton cord. Cut 18 cords each 1.5 m long. You will also need 6-9 curtain rings in mixed sizes (3-5 cm diameter); wooden or metal rings both work. Mount the wooden dowel horizontally on your work board using T-pins or hang it from an S-hook at working height.

Before mounting any cord, thread the curtain rings onto the cords that will hold them: slide 2 rings (one large, one medium) onto 2 of the cords for the left column, 2 rings onto 2 cords for the centre column, and 2-3 rings onto cords for the right column. The rings will hang free along the cord until the
gathering knot
locks them in place.
Method
Step 1: Mount all 18 cords onto the dowel using
lark's head knots

at their midpoints. You now have 36 working ends. Space the cords evenly, leaving a small gap between the three groups of 6 cords (left, centre, right columns) and between individual cords within each group.
Step 2: Work 3-4 rows of
square knots
on each group of 6 cords (treat as one group of 6, with the outer 2 on each side as working cords and the inner 2 as
filler cords
). Keep knots tight and evenly spaced.

Step 3: Slide the first curtain ring up to the position just below the knot section (approximately 10-12 cm from the dowel). Position the ring so that two cords from the group pass through the ring centre; the remaining cords go around the outside of the ring. Bind immediately below the ring with a gathering knot wrapping all 6 cords: this locks the ring in place.
Step 4: Continue with 3-4 more rows of square knots below the ring, then repeat Step 3 for the second ring on the same column, placing it 10-12 cm below the first gathering knot.
Step 5: Repeat Steps 2-4 for the centre and right columns. Stagger the ring positions slightly across the three columns if preferred, so rings at different heights create a tiered display.
Step 6: Finish each column with a final gathering knot. Trim the fringe ends at equal length (approximately 8-10 cm below the final gathering knot) or unravel the cord ends into a brushed fringe.

Step 7: Screw 2-3 small cup hooks into the underside of the wooden dowel between the columns. These hold individual earrings or small pendant necklaces. Hang the finished piece from a wall nail or hook.
Troubleshooter

attrs.problem: Curtain rings slip downward after use

content.text: The gathering knot was not tight enough to hold the ring under the weight of jewellery. Retie the gathering knot with at least 10 wraps and ensure the binding cord end is buried back through the wrap before trimming.

Tension varied between columns. Square knots should be worked at the same tightness throughout. Measure from the dowel to the first ring position on each column before tying the ring gathering knot to keep all three columns aligned.
